verdicts.reason: This is a mythic-relational claim (the creator emerging from uHlanga, the reed-source), not a LINGUISTIC cognate. Tier 1 requires proven common descent by sound-law between two words/figures. 'Unkulunkulu' (reduplication of -khulu, 'great/old') and 'uHlanga' (reed/source) are NOT etymologically the same word; they are a narrative pair within one cosmogony. Down-tier to 3 (structural/mythic relation). Keep the seam, fix the tier.

Defensible at tier 2. This is a DOCUMENTED internal ambiguity of the Nguni high-god as recorded in actual testimonies (Callaway's 19th-c. collections show the two names treated sometimes as one being, sometimes father/son). That is an attested textual fact about the recorded tradition, not merely a resemblance. Tier 2 holds.

Not a linguistic cognate. 'Unwabu' (the chameleon of the origin-of-death myth) and 'Unkulunkulu' are unrelated words; the link is that Unkulunkulu sends the chameleon — a narrative/functional relation within the death-myth. Tier 1 is wrong. Down-tier to 3.

Not a sound-law cognate. 'Amadlozi' (the ancestral shades) and 'Unkulunkulu' are distinct lexical items; the claim is the conceptual continuity that the creator is the first/greatest ancestor. That is a structural/theological relation within Nguni thought, not proven common linguistic descent. Tier 1 unjustified; down-tier to 3.
